article:
Chunk # 0.
ANALYTICAL BIOCHEMISTRY 69, 545-55 1 (1975) 
An Improved Method for Isolation of Mitochondria from 
Chick Breast Muscle Using Nagarse 
J H THAKAR AND C R ASHMORE' 
Laboratory of Muscle Biology, Department of Animal Science, 
University of California, Davis, California 9.5616 
Received March 21, 1975; accepted June 10, 1975 
An improved procedure to isolate mitochondria from chick pectoralis muscle is 
described The muscle is digested with the proteinase Nagarse and homogenized 
using a Teflon pestle Mitochondria are isolated by differential centrifugation 
These organelles are able to utilize a variety of substrates with a higher degree of 
respiratory control than mitochondria isolated without Nagarse Contrary to 
previous reports, mitochondria isolated by this method from chick pectoralis 
muscle (crW fibers) are able to oxidize the substrate /3-hydroxybutyrate with good 
respiratory control 
There are several methods available for the isolation of skeletal 
muscle mitochondria from various sources like rat, hamster, pigeon, 
human etc (l-l 1) Most of these methods are quite reliable for ob- 
taining mitochondria with good structural and functional integrity Chick 
pectoralis (breast) muscle has been a difficult muscle from which to 
isolate mitochondria (2), probably because more than 95% of its fibers 
are white (crW) (12., 13) This type of muscle fiber is known to have rela- 
tively few mitochondria (14) Some studies are reported on pectoralis 
muscle mitochondria from chicks (2,12,15,16), but only those from this 
laboratory (2,12) give any detailed information about the critical indices 
of oxidative phosphorylation such as respiratory control and ADP/O 
ratios The present study is an extension of our attempts to improve the 
method for isolation of mitochondria from anerobic muscle such as the 
chick pectoralis muscle 
In our earlier attempts to isolate mitochondria from chick muscle 
(2,12,13), organelles were isolated using a medium similar to that used 
by Chappell and Perry (8), the major difference being that we increased 
the osmolarity of the medium to the equivalent of 0.44 M sucrose by ad- 
dition of 0.1 M sucrose to the medium In the present study bacterial 
proteinase, Nagarse, was incorporated into the isolation medium as 
described below 
1 This work was supported in part by NIH Grant AM 16716-02 
545 
COpyright 0 1975 by Academic Press, Inc 
All tights of reproduction in any form reserved 546 THAKAR AND ASHMORE 
MATERIALS 
Normal chicks (line-200), 3-5 weeks old, were used for this study 
They were hatched at the Department of Avian Science, University of 
California, Davis, and were maintained in our laboratory (2) This line of 
chicks has been described by Holliday ef al (17) All the substrates and 
other chemicals including BSA (bovine serum albumin) ADP, and ATP 
were purchased from Sigma Chemical Company (St Louis, MO.) Na- 
garse (lyophilized bacterial proteinase) was a product of Nagarse and 
Company, Ltd., Osaka, Japan 
METHODS 
Isolation of Mitochondria 
Chicks were decapitated using a guillotine Superficial pectoralis 
muscles were removed and placed in ice-cold isolation medium (0.1 M 
sucrose, 0.05 M Tris-HCl, 0.005 M MgCl,, 0.01 M EDTA, 0.18 M KCl, 
pH 7.4) The rest of the procedure to isolate mitochondria was carried 
out at 0-4°C The muscle was rapidly cleaned by removing any visible 
connective tissue or fat It then was minced using two scalpels Approxi- 
mately 5 g of minced tissue was incubated at 0-4°C for 5 min in 50 ml 
of isolation medium containing 0.001 M ATP, 1% BSA, and 5 mg of 
Nagarse The optimum amount of Nagarse was selected by preliminary 
trials Chicken muscle used in the present study has a softer consistency 
than hamster, dog, and rat adductor or gastrocnemius muscles, hence 
only 1 mg of Nagarse/g of tissue was required compared to 5 mg used 
previously (1) The incubation was followed by gentle homogenization 
with a Teflon pestle (0.66-mm clearance), making five complete passes 
The homogenate was then incubated for an additional 5 min on ice, with 
frequent stirring Then an equal volume of isolation medium containing 
BSA and ATP but no Nagarse was added to the homogenate The 
resulting suspension was homogenized as before The final homogenate 
was centrifuged at 480g in a Sorvall RC2-B refrigerated centrifuge for 
10 min The supernatant fluid was centrifuged at 8700g for 10 min to 
harvest the mitochondrial pellet The pellet was then washed by sus- 
pending in 10 ml of isolation medium and collected by centrifuging at 
8700 for 5 min The resulting mitochondrial pellet was finally suspended 
in medium containing 0.3 M mannitol, 0.01 M KCl, 0.01 M KH2P04, and 
0.01 M Tris-HCI at pH 7.4 The protein concentration of this suspen- 
sion was about 15-20 mg/ml 
For a comparison purpose, mitochondria were also isolated by using 
the same procedure but without Nagarse In some experiments mi- 
tochondria were isolated using a Polytron homogenizer

mitochondria.protocol:
**Steps:**

1. Chicks were decapitated and superficial pectoralis muscles were removed.
2. Muscles were placed in ice-cold isolation medium (0.1 M sucrose, 0.05 M Tris-HCl, 0.005 M MgCl2, 0.01 M EDTA, 0.18 M KCl, pH 7.4).
3. Visible connective tissue and fat were removed from the muscle.
4. The muscle was minced using two scalpels.
5. Approximately 5 g of minced tissue was incubated at 0-4°C for 5 min in 50 ml of isolation medium containing 0.001 M ATP, 1% BSA, and 5 mg of Nagarse.
6. The incubation was followed by gentle homogenization with a Teflon pestle (0.66-mm clearance), making five complete passes.
7. The homogenate was then incubated for an additional 5 min on ice, with frequent stirring.
8. An equal volume of isolation medium containing BSA and ATP but no Nagarse was added to the homogenate.
9. The resulting suspension was homogenized as before.
10. The final homogenate was centrifuged at 480g for 10 min.
11. The supernatant fluid was centrifuged at 8700g for 10 min to harvest the mitochondrial pellet.
12. The pellet was washed by suspending in 10 ml of isolation medium and collected by centrifuging at 8700g for 5 min.
13. The resulting mitochondrial pellet was finally suspended in medium containing 0.3 M mannitol, 0.01 M KCl, 0.01 M KH2PO4, and 0.01 M Tris-HCl at pH 7.4.
14. The protein concentration of this suspension was about 15-20 mg/ml.
15. For comparison purposes, mitochondria were also isolated without Nagarse using the same procedure.
16. In some experiments, mitochondria were isolated using a Polytron homogenizer.

**Incompleteness:**
- The article does not provide information on the duration of the incubation.
- The article does not specify the speed or duration of the centrifugation steps.
- The article does not mention the temperature at which the final suspension is stored.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating mitochondria from chick pectoralis muscle using Nagarse. The steps mentioned cover the major aspects of the isolation process, including tissue preparation, enzyme digestion, homogenization, and centrifugation. However, there are some missing details, such as the incubation duration, centrifugation speed and duration, and storage temperature of the final suspension. These gaps in the protocol may require further experimentation or consultation with the authors for clarification.
mitochondria.organisms:
**Organisms Mentioned in the Mitochondrial Extraction Protocol:**
- Chicks

summary: Summary 4: In a paper published in Analytical Biochemistry, J.H. Thakar and C.R. Ashmore present an improved method for isolating mitochondria from chick breast muscle using Nagarse. The muscle is digested with Nagarse and homogenized with a Teflon pestle, followed by isolation through differential centrifugation. The resulting mitochondrial pellet, with a protein concentration of 15-20 mg/ml, demonstrates higher respiratory control and efficient oxidation of /3-hydroxybutyrate. This improved methodology ensures the reliable isolation of mitochondria, removing connective tissue and fat, and allowing for the study of respiratory control and oxidative phosphorylation in skeletal muscle mitochondria.

Variety of substrates with a higher degree of respiratory control
Skeletal muscle mitochondria from various sources
Rest of the procedure to isolate mitochondria
Rapidly cleaned by removing any visible connective tissue or fat
Optimum amount of Nagarse was selected by preliminary trials
Incubation was followed by gentle homogenization
Supernatant fluid was centrifuged to harvest the mitochondrial pellet
Pellet was then washed by suspending in isolation medium
Resulting mitochondrial pellet was finally suspended
Protein concentration of this suspension was about 15-20 mg/ml
Mitochondria were also isolated by using the same procedure but without Nagarse
In some experiments mitochondria were isolated using a Polytron homogenizer
J H Thakar and C R Ashmore
Improved Methodology for Isolation of Mitochondria

article:
Chunk # 1.
CHICK MUSCLE MITOCHONDRIA 547 
Oxidative Phosphorylation 
Oxygen consumption by mitochondria was measured polarographi- 
tally with a Clark electrode (Yellow Springs Instrument Company) at 
26°C using a Gilson Model .KM oxygraph Approximately 0.5-l .O mg of 
mitochondrial protein was added to the oxygen cuvette having 1.3 ml of 
medium containing 0.3 M mannitol, 0.01 M KCl, 0.01 M KH,PO,, and 
0.01 M Tris-HCl at pH 7.4 plus 1% BSA The final concentrations of 
substrates were 6 mM except for L-malate which was used at 1.2 mM 
and L-glutamate which was used at 8 mM Special care was taken to see 
that there was no air-bubble left in the oxygen cuvette or in the delivery 
tube of the stopper The respiratory control ratios (RCR) and ADP/O 
ratios were calculated according to Chance and Williams (18) ADP con- 
centration was determined spectrophotometrically using a molecular 
coefficient of 15.4 at 259-nm wavelength ( 19) Mitochondrial protein 
was determined by the method of Lowry et al (20) 
RESULTS 
Table 1 presents the data obtained from the polarographic study of mi- 
tochondria isolated from pectoralis muscles from the chick using 
Nagarse as described above RCR and ADP/O ratios presented for 
various substrates are mean values of four to six preparations For each 
preparation mitochondrial oxygen consumption was stimulated four to 
five times by adding ADP The values of the first and last cycles of ox- 
ygen consumption stimulated by ADP addition are not used to calculate 
the data Oxygen consumption in the absence and presence of ADP are 
conventionally defined as state 4 and state 3 respiration rates The ratio 
of state 3 to state 4 rate is RCR The values of RCR and ADP/O ratios 
TABLE 1 
OXIDATIVE PHOSPHORYLATION OF CHICK PECTORALIS MUSCLE 
MITOCHONDRIA ISOLATED BY NAGARSE METHOD 
Substrate 0, rateD RCR* ADPIO 
Pyruvate-malate 30.0 f 6.0c 5.2 -e 1.1 2.4 2 0.1 
Succinate 37.8 k 5.0 4.1 2 0.7 1.8 f 0.3 
a-Glycerophosphate 53.2 2 9.5 4.5 2 0.8 1.5 + 0.2 
/3-Hydroxybutyrate 30.4 f 7.2 4.6 2 0.6 2.0 2 0.2 
L-Glutamate 46.0 f 8.1 7.5 ” 1.0 2.4 k 0.2 
Oxaloacetate 16.4 2 4.3 3.3 k 0.5 1.8 k 0.2 
a State 3 oxygen consumption rates expressed as micromoles of oxygen consumed per 
minute per gram of mitochondrial protein 
b RCR, state 3 oxygen consumption rate + state 4 oxygen consumption rate 
e Experimental details are given in text Each value is the mean f SE of four to six 
preparations 548 THAKAR AND ASHMORE 
FIG 1 A typical record of polarographic measurement of oxygen consumption by chick 
pectoralis muscle mitochondria The oxygen consumption was stimulated by the addition 
of ADP The numbers along the tracing represent oxygen consumption rate (state 3) 
expressed as micromoles of O2 per minute per gram of mitochondrial protein RCR, respi- 
ratory control ratio Additions: M, mitochondria; P/M, pyruvate plus malate Details given 
in the text 
are considered to be the best criteria of mitochondrial integrity These 
values reported in Table 1 are high and in an acceptable normal range 
for skeletal muscle mitochondria Figure 1 represents a typical polaro- 
graphic recording of chick pectoralis muscle mitochondrial preparation 
In an earlier paper from this laboratory (2), high RCR values were 
reported for various substrates However, it has been found that those 
values were falsely high because the measurements previously obtained 
were carried out in a cuvette of 1.5 ml volume but filled with only 1 .O ml 
of medium As a result of this an air bubble was present in the incuba- 
tion chamber This influenced the state 3 and state 4 respiration rate 
because each rate measured is the resultant of the rate of oxygen utiliza- 
tion by mitochondria and the rate at which oxygen diffuses into the 
medium In practice this had a greater effect on the state 4 oxygen con- 
sumption rate because the oxygen consumption in state 4 is low, re- 
sulting in falsely high RCR values 
Data obtained in the comparison of mitochondria prepared from chick 
pectoralis muscle using either a Teflon pestle or a Polytron homogenizer 
but without Nagarse are presented in Table 2 In both of these cases 
isolated mitochondria had lower RCR and ADP/O ratios Several of 
these preparations were uncoupled 
DISCUSSION 
There are various methods available for isolating mitochondria from 
skeletal muscle of different types and origin (1- 11)

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Measure oxygen consumption by mitochondria using a Clark electrode at 26°C.
2. Add approximately 0.5-1 mg of mitochondrial protein to the oxygen cuvette containing 1.3 ml of medium.
3. The medium contains 0.3 M mannitol, 0.01 M KCl, 0.01 M KH2PO4, and 0.01 M Tris-HCl at pH 7.4, plus 1% BSA.
4. The final concentrations of substrates are 6 mM, except for L-malate (1.2 mM) and L-glutamate (8 mM).
5. Ensure there are no air bubbles in the oxygen cuvette or delivery tube of the stopper.
6. Calculate respiratory control ratios (RCR) and ADP/O ratios according to Chance and Williams.
7. Determine ADP concentration spectrophotometrically at a wavelength of 259 nm.
8. Determine mitochondrial protein using the method of Lowry et al.
9. The data obtained from the polarographic study of mitochondria isolated from chick pectoralis muscles using Nagarse are presented in Table 1.
10. RCR and ADP/O ratios are mean values of four to six preparations.
11. Stimulate mitochondrial oxygen consumption four to five times by adding ADP.
12. Exclude the values of the first and last cycles of oxygen consumption stimulated by ADP from the data calculation.
13. Define oxygen consumption in the absence and presence of ADP as state 4 and state 3 respiration rates.
14. Calculate the ratio of state 3 to state 4 rate as RCR.
15. The values of RCR and ADP/O ratios are considered criteria for mitochondrial integrity.
16. The values reported in Table 1 are high and within an acceptable normal range for skeletal muscle mitochondria.
17. Figure 1 shows a typical polarographic recording of chick pectoralis muscle mitochondrial preparation.
18. Previous measurements carried out in a cuvette of 1.5 ml volume but filled with only 1.0 ml of medium resulted in falsely high RCR values due to the presence of an air bubble in the incubation chamber.
19. Data obtained from the comparison of mitochondria prepared from chick pectoralis muscle using a Teflon pestle or a Polytron homogenizer but without Nagarse are presented in Table 2.
20. Mitochondria prepared without Nagarse had lower RCR and ADP/O ratios, and some preparations were uncoupled.

**Conclusion:** The article provides steps for measuring oxygen consumption by mitochondria and presents data from a polarographic study of mitochondria isolated from chick pectoralis muscles. The steps for mitochondrial isolation using Nagarse are not explicitly mentioned, leaving a gap in the protocol. The article also mentions the comparison of mitochondria prepared using different methods, but the steps for these methods are not provided.

summary: Summary 4: Oxygen consumption in chick muscle mitochondria was measured using a Clark electrode from Yellow Springs Instrument Company. The experiment used mannitol as the medium and various substrates including pyruvate-malate, succinate, alpha-glycerophosphate, and beta-hydroxybutyrate. Respiratory control ratios (RCR) were calculated to assess mitochondrial integrity. State 3 and state 4 respiration rates were determined, and ADP addition induced state 3 respiration. High RCR values were observed for the substrates tested. The measurements were carried out in a 1.5 ml cuvette filled with 1 ml of medium, which affected the state 4 respiration rate and resulted in falsely high RCR values. Mitochondrial integrity was also assessed using different homogenization methods, which showed lower RCR and ADP/O ratios in mitochondria prepared without Nagarse. References: Chance and Williams (18), Lowry et al (20), Thakar and Ashmore (548).

The respiratory control ratios (RCR) and ADP/O ratios were calculated according to Chance and Williams
ADP concentration was determined spectrophotometrically using a molecular coefficient of 15.4 at 259-nm wavelength
Mitochondrial protein was determined by the method of Lowry et al
RCR and ADP/O ratios presented for various substrates are mean values of four to six preparations
Oxygen consumption in the absence and presence of ADP are conventionally defined as state 4 and state 3 respiration rates
The ratio of state 3 to state 4 rate is RCR

Measurements previously obtained were carried out in a cuvette of 1.5 ml volume but filled with only 1 .O ml of medium

Isolated mitochondria had lower RCR and ADP/O ratios

article:
Chunk # 2.
In the past, several 
of these have been tested in our laboratory with varying degrees of suc- TABLE 2 
2 OXIDATIVE PHOSPHORYLATION OF CHICK PECTORALIS MUSCLE MITOCHONDRIA ISOLATED BY 
POLYTRON AND TEFLON HOMOGENIZATION WITHOUT NAGARSE~ i 
Teflon Polytron 5 
% 
Substrate 0, rate RCR ADPIO 0, rate RCR ADP/O L 
Pyruvate-malate 36.0 2 8.1 2.0 2 0.3 1.7 2 0.4 27.0 + 7.6 4.0 + 1.0 1.3 k 0.6 5 
Succinate 28.8 k 5.2 2.5 2 0.4 1.6 f 0.2 19.6 2 6.5 1.0 0 d 
cY-Glycerophosphate - - - 19.4 T 5.8 1.8 f 0.3 0.9 + 0.2 
P-Hydroxybutyrate* 28.3 1.8 1.4 2 
17.7 2 7.1 1.0 0 2 
’ Details are given under Table 1 and in the text z 
* Only one preparation was coupled 5 550 THAKAR AND ASHMORE 
cess For example, mitochondria from chick pectoralis muscle prepared 
and assayed according to Max et al (7) are completely uncoupled The 
Nagarse method of preparing mitochondria has been criticized by 
various workers (7,9,2 l), but other laboratories have found it very useful 
for isolating mitochondria from skeletal muscle with consistently high 
RCR values and ADP/O ratios (1,3,10) Bullock et al (5) have even 
prepared good quality mitochondria by proteolytic digestion of muscle 
without applying any mechanical homogenization 
The use of BSA and EDTA in media to isolate mitochondria from 
skeletal muscle has received a general recognition BSA may protect mi- 
tochondria by binding free fatty acids and inhibiting lipase hydrolysis 
(22) EDTA may function as a Ca*+ chelator whereby preventing the 
Ca*+-overload and related injury to mitochondria It may also act as an- 
tioxidant and prevent lipid peroxidation of mitochondrial membranes 
(23) The low concentration of sucrose in conjunction with KC1 used in 
the present study may prevent the formation of a gelatinous consistency 
of the homogenate and facilitate a better separation of the mitochondrial 
fraction (11) 
In this study, contrary to a previous report (12), mitochondria ob- 
tained from the pectoralis muscle using Nagarse during the isolation 
precedure actively oxidized the substrate p-hydroxybutyrate, and with 
good respiratory control When mitochondria from this muscle fiber type 
are isolated without Nagarse, they exhibit respiratory control with some 
substrates but virtually no control when /3-hydroxybutyrate is the sub- 
strate Kark et al (24) found that mitochondria isolated from “white” 
guinea pig muscle have very low P-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase 
activity, and that both mitochondria and slices of “white” muscle ox- 
idize p-hydroxybutyrate at low rates compared to those of “red” muscle 
However, we have found (unpublished) that mitochondria isolated from 
pectoralis (white) muscle of the chick, using the Nagarse method, ox- 
idize /3-hydroxybutyrate with equal efficiency with those isolated from 
“red” muscles We previously reported (2) that the osmolarity of the 
isolation medium can affect the in vitro performance of “white” muscle 
mitochondria differentially 
These findings demonstrate that the methods employed during the 
isolation procedure can have a selective and a significant affect on the in 
vitro performance of the organelles from different types of muscle and 
point out the hazards involved when attempting to relate the in vitro per- 
formance observed with the physiological function of mitochondria in 
vivo 
Finally, it is worth noting that mitochondria isolated in this study 
using Nagarse were stable for 3-4 hr, whereas mitochondria isolated 
without Nagarse were not stable for more than 0.5 hr after isolation CHICK MUSCLE MITOCHONDRIA 551

Therefore, it is not possible to extract the mitochondrial isolation steps accurately.

summary: Summary 5: Laboratory experiments on chick pectoralis muscle used Polytron and Teflon homogenization techniques. Mitochondria were isolated without Nagarse, remaining stable for 3-4 hours. Low sucrose and KC1 concentrations prevented gelatinous consistency during homogenization. BSA and EDTA were used in the media to protect mitochondria and prevent lipid peroxidation. The osmolarity of the isolation medium affected the performance of mitochondria from different muscle types. The isolation methods significantly impacted organelle performance. Researchers have reported varying degrees of success with different isolation methods.
clean_entities: Isolated by Polytron and Teflon homogenization without Nagarse
Details are given under Table 1 and in the text
Mitochondria from chick pectoralis muscle prepared and assayed according to Max et al (7) are completely uncoupled
The Nagarse method of preparing mitochondria has been criticized by various workers (7
Other laboratories have found it very useful for isolating mitochondria from skeletal muscle with consistently high RCR values and ADP/O ratios (1
Bullock et al (5) have even prepared good quality mitochondria by proteolytic digestion of muscle without applying any mechanical homogenization
The use of BSA and EDTA in media to isolate mitochondria from skeletal muscle has received a general recognition
BSA may protect mitochondria by binding free fatty acids and inhibiting lipase hydrolysis (22)
EDTA may function as a Ca2+ chelator
EDTA may also act as an antioxidant and prevent lipid peroxidation of mitochondrial membranes (23)

The low concentration of sucrose in conjunction with KC1 used in the present study may prevent the formation of a gelatinous consistency of the homogenate and facilitate a better separation of the mitochondrial fraction (11)
When mitochondria from this muscle fiber type are isolated without Nagarse
Kark et al (24) found that mitochondria isolated from "white" guinea pig muscle have very low p-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase activity
We previously reported (2) that the osmolarity of the isolation medium can affect the in vitro performance of "white" muscle mitochondria differentially

These findings demonstrate that the methods employed during the isolation procedure can have a selective and significant effect on the in vitro performance of the organelles from different types of muscle
The hazards involved when attempting to relate the in vitro performance observed with the physiological function of mitochondria in vivo
Mitochondria isolated in this study using Nagarse were stable for 3-4 hr
dirty_entities: preventing Ca2+-overload and related injury to mitochondria
mitochondria obtained from the pectoralis muscle using Nagarse during the isolation procedure actively oxidized the substrate p-hydroxybutyrate
they exhibit respiratory control with some substrates but virtually no control when p-hydroxybutyrate is the substrate
and that both mitochondria and slices of "white" muscle oxidize p-hydroxybutyrate at low rates compared to those of "red" muscle
we have found (unpublished) that mitochondria isolated from pectoralis (white) muscle of the chick
oxidize p-hydroxybutyrate with equal efficiency with those isolated from "red" muscles
whereas mitochondria isolated without Nagarse were not stable for more than 0.5 hr after isolation
B. (1961) Proc 5rh Znt Congr Biochem MOSCOW 5
L. (1974) Proc Sot Exp Biol Med 146
V. (1968) Proc Sot Exp Bio/ Med 129

**Conclusion:**

All the articles mentioned the isolation of mitochondria using the method of differential centrifugation. However, no detailed steps or additional information were provided in any of the articles. The protocol gaps include the specific centrifugation speeds, buffer compositions, and other relevant details necessary for a complete mitochondrial isolation protocol.
